topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure, thick and smelling faintly of fresh ink, lay crumpled on the kitchen counter. I picked it up, my fingers brushing the slick paper as if it were contaminated. Another 'upgrade,' they chirped in the fine print. I stared out the window at the aging brick facade of the building, the familiar, slightly peeling paint mocking the glossy promises of stainless steel and curated communal spaces. A low, almost inaudible sigh escaped my lips, a sound heavy with weary resignation. I pushed the brochure back onto the counter, the small movement sharp and dismissive. This whole transition felt like an imposition, a forced rebranding of a place that had, however imperfectly, been home. I traced a crack in the tile floor, my gaze lingering on the imperfections, finding a strange comfort in the familiar flaws.

[story 2]

"Condo." The word echoed in my head, hollow and sharp. I leaned against the doorframe of my cramped living room, the cheap veneer protesting slightly under my weight. I watched the mail slot, anticipating the inevitable deluge of developer flyers and mandatory orientation schedules. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck bunching. I picked up my phone, not to call a friend, but to scroll through listings for places I wouldn't consider, just to remind myself what 'space' felt like before this seismic shift. Every planned amenity—the rooftop pool, the concierge service—registered only as an added layer of unwelcome intrusion. I let out a short, humorless exhale, shaking my head slowly, as if trying to dislodge a persistent, irritating film from my vision.

[story 3]

I stood in the hallway, the air thick with the scent of old carpet and stale coffee. The notice, framed starkly against the worn wallpaper, was undeniable. Condominium conversion. My eyes scanned the text, not absorbing the details, but cataloging the changes: designated parking, shared maintenance fees, the sudden imposition of rules. A cold knot settled beneath my ribs. I walked toward the stairs, my steps deliberate and heavy, each footfall a measured refusal to fully embrace the prospect. I paused at the landing, turning back to look at the worn banister. There was a deep, ingrained sense of ownership here, a history etched into the very structure, and this shiny new veneer felt like a cheap, ill-fitting costume. I simply shook my head, a small, tight movement, and continued my ascent.
